data.title: Aurora Innovation: Commercialization Approaches, But Potholes Still Exist
data.description:
After a slow start as a public company, Aurora Innovation, Inc. (NASDAQ:AUR) is finally aggressively pursuing commercialization of driverless truck technology. Unlike other pre-revenue firms, the stock has maintained a rich valuation heading into the much anticipated launch event. My investment thesis remains Bearish on the stock as the ramp up to material revenues is still years away and uncertain.

April Launch

Aurora Innovation is quickly driving towards an April launch of their driverless truck business starting with one truck running the Dallas to Houston truck lane. The company plans to ramp up to 10 driverless trucks on this lane before launching the Ft. north to El Paso lane in Q225.

The key here is that Aurora Innovation is far more aggressive in launching service, though the launch of the first lane was delayed from a 2024 target and the service still won't operate at night and in bad weather. The driverless truck business could have operations from Houston to Phoenix by the end of 2025 in q quick ramp of a second lane.

The Aurora Driver business has only reached 88% autonomous loads without on-site interaction, but the company is moving forward as the service is operational with limited on-site help. Aurora Innovation estimates that the driverless truck service will reach 90% API (Autonomy Performance Indicator) by the time of launch.

The API is a measure of how many loads are operated without on-site assistance. After service launch, Aurora Innovation will have far more production miles to better refine the Aurora Driver product.

Ultimately, the company will expand away from the current terminal model and include bad weather driving. The big opportunity for replacing human drivers comes with the expansion to lanes that stretch beyond 600 miles where human drivers have to rest.

Extreme Valuation Already

The biggest issue now is the stock valuation and the heavy investments to get the service off the ground. Aurora Innovation spent nearly $200 million on operating expenses in the last quarter and nearly $800 million for the year with the negative cash flows at $645 million for the year.

The company ended the year with a cash and investments balance of ~$1.2 billion. Due to limited revenue in the next year during the commercial launch with only up to 10 trucks running on the initial lane, Aurora Innovation has another couple of years of heavy losses ahead with a goal of only reaching a positive gross profit margin by 2026.

The business will likely need to raise additional capital, though the current market valuation will limit any further dilution. Aurora Innovation has a market cap of $16.5 billion with 1.72 billion shares outstanding.

The company forecasts quarterly cash burn in the $175 to $185 million range, hence a path towards being funded into the 2H26. Aurora Innovation would burn at least $700 million in 2025, leaving the company with a cash balance of $500 million to start 2026.

Analyst estimates forecast the company reaching $330 million in 2027 revenues. Aurora Innovation will charge somewhere around $2 to $3 per mile to operate loads an eventually transfer to a driver as a service model and charge a fee representative of the human driver costs at $0.91 in 2023.

Based on the Investor Day 2024 estimates, the company guided to 2028 revenues reaching $1.3 billion. Aurora Innovation has a long pathway to generating those type of revenues requiring the Aurora Driver to complete 2 billion miles worth of trips at what would amount to $0.65 per mile driven.

Aurora Innovation has a long way to go from 10 trucks on a single truck lane by mid-year to reach upwards of 10,000 trucks driving 200K+ miles a year. The stock is just far too expensive here at nearly 13x a perfect scenario revenue situation for 4 years from now despite the service not even being launched yet.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that investors should use the recent gains to cash out of the stock. Aurora Innovation is making huge strides in driverless technology, but the company is still 12-18 months away from a real inflection point for the actual revenue portion of the business. The stock has already fully valued in the business meeting these large growth targets without assuming any of the risk and the future dilution potential.

data.title: Return On Equity, Earnings Yield And Market Efficiency: Back To Basics
data.description:
While I was working on my last two data updates for 2025, I got sidetracked, as I am wont to do, by two events. The first was the response that I received to my last data update, where I looked at the profitability of businesses, and specifically at how a comparison of accounting returns on equity (capital) to costs of equity (capital) can yield a measure of excess returns. The second was a comment that I made on a LinkedIn post that had built on my implied equity premium approach to the Indian market but had run into a roadblock because of an assumption that, in an efficient market, the return on equity would equate to the cost of equity. I pointed to the flaw in the logic, but the comments thereafter suggested such deep confusion about what returns on equity or capital measure, and what comprises an efficient market, that I think it does make sense to go back to basics and see if some of the confusion can be cleared up.

The Lead In: Business Formation

To keep this example as stripped of complexity as I can, at least to begin, I will start with two entrepreneurs who invest $60 million apiece to start new businesses, albeit with very different economics:

  • The first entrepreneur starts business A, with a $60 million investment up front, and that business is expected to generate $15 million in net income every year in perpetuity.
  • The second entrepreneur starts business B, again with a $60 million investment up front, and that investment is expected to generate $3 million every year in perpetuity.

With these characteristics, the accounting balance sheets for these companies will be identical right after they start up, and the book value of equity will be $60 million in each company.

The return on equity is an entirely accounting concept, and it can be computed by dividing the net income of each of the two businesses by the book value of equity:

  • Return on equity for Business A = Net income for Business A / Book Value of Equity for Business A = 15/60 = 25%
  • Return on equity for Business B = Net income for Business B / Book Value of Equity for Business B = 3/60 = 5%

Assume that both these businesses have the same underlying business risk that translates into a cost of equity of 10%, giving the two businesses the following excess returns:

  • Excess Return for Business A = Return on equity for Business A – Cost of equity for Business A = 25% -10% = 15%
  • Excess Return for Business B = Return on equity for Business B – Cost of equity for Business B = 5% -10% = -5%

In the language of my last post, the first business is a good one, because it creates value by earning more than your money would have earned elsewhere on an investment of equivalent risk, and the second is a bad one, because it does not.

The return on equity may be an equation that comes from accounting statements, but in keeping with my argument that every number needs a narrative, each of these numbers has a narrative, often left implicit, that should be made explicit.

  • On business A, the story has to be one of strong barriers to entry that allow it to sustain its excess returns in perpetuity, and those could include anything from a superlative brand name to patent protection to exclusive access to a natural resource. In the absence of these competitive advantages, these excess returns would have faded very quickly over time.
  • On business B, you have a challenge, since it does seem irrational that an entrepreneur would enter a bad business, and while that irrationality cannot be ruled out (perhaps the entrepreneur thinks that earning any profit makes for a good business), the reality is that outside events can wreak havoc on the bet paid plans of businesses. For instance, it is possible that the entrepreneur’s initial expectations were that he or she would earn much more than 5%, but a competitor launching a much better product or a regulatory change could have changed those expectations.

In sum, the return on equity and its more expansive variant, the return on invested capital, measure what a company is making on the capital it has invested in business, and is a measure of business quality.

The Market Launch

Assume now that the owners of both businesses (A and B) list their businesses in the market, disclosing what they expect to generate as net income in perpetuity. Investors in equity markets will now get a chance to price the two companies, and if markets are efficient, they will arrive at the following:

Thus, a discerning (efficient) market would value business A, with $15 million in net income in perpetuity at $150 million, while valuing business B, with $3 million in net income in perpetuity, at $30 million. If you are wondering why you would discount net income, rather than cash flow, the unique features of these investments (constant net income, no growth and forever lives) makes net income equal to cash flow.

Even with this very simplistic example, there are useful implications. The first is that if markets are efficient, the price to book ratios will reflect the quality of these companies. In this example, for instance, business A, with a market value of equity of $150 million and a book value of equity of $60 million, will trade at 2.50 times book value, whereas company B with a market value of equity of $30 million and a book value of equity of $60 million will trade at half of book value. Both companies would be fairly valued, though the first trades at well above book value and the second at well below, thus explaining why a lazy variant of value investing, built almost entirely on buying stocks that trade at low price to book ratio,, will lead you to holding bad businesses, not undervalued ones.

As I noted at the start of this post, it was motivated by trying to clear up a fundamental misunderstanding of what return on equity measures. In fact, the working definition that some commenters used for return on equity was obtained by dividing the net income by the market value of equity. That is not return on equity, but an earnings to price ratio, i.e., the earnings yield, and in these examples, with no growth and perpetual (constant) net income, that earnings yield will be equal to the cost of equity in an efficient market.

Extending the Discussion

One of the advantages of this very simple illustration is that it now can be used as a launching pad for casting light on some of the most interesting questions in investing:

  1. Good companies versus Good Investments: I have written about the contrast between a good company and a good investment, and this example provides an easy way to illustrate the difference. Looking at companies A and B, there is absolutely no debating the fact that company A is better company, with sustainable moats and high returns on equity (25%), than company B, which struggles to make money (return on equity of 5%), and clearly is in a bad business. However, which of these two companies is the better investment rests entirely on how the market prices them: As you can see, the good company (A) can be a good, bad or neutral investment, depending on whether its is priced at less than, greater than or equal to its fair value ($150 million) and the same can be said about the bad company (B), with the price relative to its fair value ($30 million). At fair value, both become neutral investments, generating returns to shareholders that match their cost of equity.
  2. The Weakest Link in Excess Returns: The excess return is computed as the difference between return on equity and the cost of equity, and while it is true that different risk and return models and differences in risk parameters (relative risk measures and equity risk premiums) can cause variations in cost of equity calculations, the return on equity is the weaker link in this comparison. To understand some of the ways the return on equity can be skewed, consider the following variants on the simple example in this case:
    • Accounting inconsistencies: As an entirely accounting number, the return on equity is exposed to accounting inconsistencies and miscategorization. To illustrate with our simple example, assume that half the money invested in business A is in R&D, which accountants expense, instead of capitalizing. That business will report a loss of $15 million (with the R&D expense of $30 million more than wiping out the profit of $15 million) in the first year on book capital of $30 million (the portion of the capital invested that is not R&D), but in the years following, it will report a return on capital of 50.00% (since net income will revert back to $15 million, and equity will stay at $30 million). Carrying this through to the real world, you should not be surprised to see technology and pharmaceutical companies, the two biggest spenders on R&D, report much higher accounting returns than they are actually earning on their investments..
    • Aging assets: In our example, we looked at firms an instant after the upfront investment was made, when the book value of investment measures what was paid for the assets acquired. As assets age, two tensions appear that can throw off book value, the first being inflation, which if not adjusted for, will result in the book value being understated, and accounting returns overstated. The other is accounting depreciation, which often has little to do with economic depreciation (value lost from aging), and subject to gaming. Extrapolating, projects and companies with older assets will tend to have overstated accounting returns, as inflation and depreciation lay waste to book values. In fact, with an aging company, and adding in stock buybacks, the book value of equity can become negative (and is negative for about 10% of the companies in my company data sample).
    • Fair Value Accounting: For the last few decades, the notion of fair value accounting has been a fever dream for accounting rule writers, and those rules, albeit in patchwork form, have found their way into corporate balance sheets. In my view, fair value accounting is pointless, and I can use my simple example to illustrate why. If you marked the assets of both company A and company B to market, you would end with book values of $150 million and $30 million for the two companies and returns on equity of 10% for both firms. In short, if fair value accounting does what it is supposed to do, every firm in the market will earn a return on equity (capital) equal to the cost of equity (capital), rendering it useless as a metric for separating good and bad businesses. If fair value accounting fails at what it is supposed to do, which is the more likely scenario, you will end up with book values of equity that measure neither original capital invested nor current market value, and returns on equity and capital that become noise.
  3. Growth enters the equation: For companies A and B, in this example, we assumed that the net income was constant, i.e., there is no growth. Introducing growth into the equation changes none of the conclusions that we have drawn so far, but it makes reading both the return on equity and the earnings yield much messier. To see why, assume that company A in the example continues to have no growth, but company B expects to see compounded annual growth of 50% a year in its net income of $3 million for the next decade. We can no longer consign company B to the bad business pile as easily, and the current earnings to price ratio for that company will no longer be equal to the cost of equity, even if markets are efficient. Incorporating growth into the analysis will also mean that net income is not equal to cash flow, since some or a large portion of that net income will have to get reinvested back to deliver the growth. In fact, this is the argument that I used in my second data update to explain why comparing the earnings yield to the treasury bond rate is unlikely to yield a complete assessment of whether stocks are under or over valued, since it ignores growth and reinvestment entirely.
  4. Exiting bad businesses: This example also helps to bring home why it is so difficult for companies in bad businesses to fix their "badness" or exit their businesses. In the case of company B, for instance, telling the manager to find projects that earn more than 10% is advice that can be freely dished out, but how exactly do you invent good projects in a business that has turned bad? While exiting the business seems to be a better choice, that presupposes that you will get your capital ($60 million) back when you do, but in the real world, potential buyers will discount that value. In fact, if you divest or sell the bad business for less than $30 million, you are actually worse off than staying in the business and continuing to generate $3 million a year in perpetuity, which has a $30 million value. In the real world, most companies in bad businesses hire new CEOs, restructure their businesses and enter new businesses in a desperate attempt to become good businesses, and enrich consultants and bankers, but not their own shareholders, along the way.

Conclusion

Many of the comments on my seventh data update, and on my explanation about why ROE and cost of equity don’t have to be equal in an efficient market, came from people with degrees and certifications in finance, and quite a few of the commenters had “finance professional” listed in their profile. Rather than take issue with them, I would argue that this misunderstanding of basics is a damning indictment of how these concepts and topics are taught in the classroom, and since I may very well be one of the culprits, one reason that I wrote this post is to remind myself that I have to revisit the basics, before making ambitious leaps into corporate financial analysis and valuation. For those of you who are not finance professionals, but rely on them for advice, I hope this is a cautionary note on taking these professionals (consultants, appraisers, bankers) at their word. Some of them throw buzzwords and metrics around, with little understanding of what they mean and how they are related, and it is caveat emptor.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: P10 Q4 Earnings: Strong Fund Raising Momentum Expected To Continue (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment thesis

Shares of P10 (NYSE:PX) have been trending higher ever since management outlined ambitious growth targets at the Investor Day last September. Subsequent quarterly results have been impressive due to management vastly outperforming with regard to Fee Paying Asset Under Management ("FPAUM") growth, along with strong profitability. While there are tough comps this year due to the outperformance in 2024, as well as lingering concerns regarding its growing accounts receivables, I find the current valuation at 12.9 times FCF very compelling for an asset manager which has a promising growth outlook. Though I was Neutral previously, I am now upgrading shares to a Buy.

Key highlights from Q4 earnings

P10 reported Q4 results where revenue of $85 million was up 35% year over year. This was boosted by $19 million in catch-up fees, without which organic growth was close to 8%. FPAUM grew 10% year over year to $25.7 billion as shown above, helped by $3.8 billion in gross fundraising during 2024, well above the $2.5 billion that was initially guided. During Q4 alone, $0.7 billion in gross FPAUM was added, 10% higher than the year ago period.

Fee-Related Earnings ("FRE") of $42.7 million in Q4 was up 39%, at a margin of 50%. FRE in FY24 was $142.1 million, up 15% year over year, at a margin of 49%. While revenue was up 23% in FY24, corresponding growth in operating expenses was only 7%, helping lift overall margins.

Solid outlook and encouraging long term growth prospects

Management guided for $4 billion in gross fundraising this year with step downs expected to be approximately $1.5 billion. Additionally I expect approximately $1.2 billion in FPAUM from the Qualitas acquisition, which is expected to close in Q1 for a purchase price of $42.25 million in cash, $20.75 million in P10 stock and $35 million in potential future earnouts. Being solely focused on Europe, Qualitas has doubled its FPAUM in the last three years.

Together with Qualitas, P10 is expected to have 19 commingled funds in the market at various times in 2025. Based on my estimation for net added FPAUM of $3.7 billion this year, I see FPAUM reaching $29.4 billion by year end, with an average FPAUM in 2025 of close to $28.5 billion. This implies total year over year growth in FPAUM of 14.3%, with organic growth being 9.7%.

Management remains on track to deliver over $50 billion in FPAUM by 2029, a target shared at the Investor Day. Meeting target shown above requires a 14% CAGR in the coming five years.

The market outlook remains favorable, with industry research showing that the private equity market is set to grow at a CAGR of 10% in the coming years, with larger and more scaled alternate asset managers space like Hamilton Lane (HLNE) and StepStone (STEP), both of which I have covered recently, are expecting to grow FPAUM in the low to high double digit rates this year.

However, a key headwind to consider in FY25, is that while FRE margins were 49% in FY24, this was helped by the higher than expected catchup fees of $39 million versus $16 million expected. Given that catchup fees is expected to be considerably lower in FY25, this will make comps much harder for revenue growth and margins. Furthermore, Qualitas is also expected to have slightly lower margins, and so it is likely that FRE margins will drop to approximately 46%. Nevertheless, management has continued to emphasize that margins should exceed 50% in the long term through operating leverage as some of its currently lower margin, faster growing funds scale up.

PX stock valuation

I will approach valuing PX shares by estimating FCF expected for FY25 and subsequently compare its valuation multiple with relevant public market peers. In line with management's guidance for an average fee rate of 103 basis points, fee related revenue should be $303 million based on my FPAUM estimates shared earlier. FRE should be close to $140 million, at a 46% margin. I will then deduct $5 million in annual capex and $25 million in interest expenses, while assuming no cash outflows for earn out payments. This results in $110 million in FCF for FY25. Notably, to be conservative I have not included the positive impact from potential catch up fees which could add further upside of at least $10 million of high margin earnings, but this remains challenging to forecast.

Following the expected completion of the Qualitas acquisition, I expect shares outstanding to increase from 111 million at the end of Q4 to 112.7 million with the company's net debt standing at $291 million following the cash consideration of $42 million. This translates to a Price/FCF multiple of 12.9.

The valuation multiple of PX is compelling when considering its promising growth outlook and margin upside, especially as Hamilton Lane and StepStone, both of which I recently rated Neutral, trade at Price/FCF multiples which are above 30. I believe this valuation discount stems from the lingering impact from the short seller report which highlighted that P10's previous co-CEOs were involved in an investigation into the misappropriation of Covid funds, which led to them stepping down and Luke Sarsfield being appointed as CEO in October 2023. Despite this, the underlying business appears to have been minimally impacted by all of this.

Risks to consider

While investors are not asked to pay a steep valuation, future returns may be negatively impacted if organic growth rates slow down due to a more challenging environment for capital raising. While P10 remains a small player in a large and competitive market, it does have competitive advantages related to the well recognized brands it owns, together with the valuable dataset of historical private market data which it has access to. While management is keen on augmenting growth through acquisitions, I would point out that P10's debt load is relatively high, with a leverage ratio of above 2, which provides limited financial flexibility to execute on large acquisitions, unless management opts to issue more stock as part of the transaction.

Another concern which I highlighted in my previous writeup is the growing accounts receivable from related parties, namely Enhanced Permanent Capital and Crossroads. This is highlighted above, and has led to a trend where working capital has been negatively impacted over the past two years, which has led to lower FCF generation. Going forward, investors should closely monitor the impacts to working capital in order to ensure that the FCF generation does not deviate too much from my estimate.

Buy PX stock

P10 is showing good momentum with respect to growing FPAUM, both organically and inorganically, while remaining on track to meet its ambitious target for 2029. Margins have remained solid as revenue growth has outpaced expense growth. While lower catch-up fees will be a headwind for revenue growth and margins this year, I find the current valuation at 12.9 times FCF too large a discount versus its peers, given the promising outlook for the business. As a result, I am upgrading PX stock to a Buy.

data.title: Trump Media: A New Business Plan
data.description:
One of the most fascinating stock stories in 2024 was Trump Media & Technology Group Corp. (NASDAQ:DJT). After finally closing a SPAC merger, the company set out to revolutionize free speech social media. While there was plenty of hype and investors celebrated after Donald Trump was re-elected, the business itself has yet to take off. Recently, Trump Media announced a major change to its overall strategy, and investors will have to keep waiting for actual results to be delivered.

Previous coverage of Trump Media:

It was about three months ago when I last took a look at Trump Media, just after the election and posting of the company's Q3 results. Revenues for the quarter were the best of any period in 2024 so far, and the year over year decline in the top line was its lowest of the three quarters. Unfortunately, the business was still only doing about $1 million in total revenue per quarter, despite the company being worth several billion dollars.

At that time, I mentioned how it was time for Trump Media to finally deliver some decent results. The hope at that point was that a streaming platform could either bring in meaningful advertising and or subscription revenue. Given the extreme numbers on traditional valuation metrics and limited results to date, I maintained a sell rating on the stock. Since then, shares have lost roughly 11%, compared to a 4% gain in the S&P 500, and they've done even worse since I first covered the name when it was still in the SPAC merger process.

DJT Q4 results and financial situation:

In an 8-k filing last week, Trump Media announced revenues of about $3.6 million for the full year. This put Q4 2024 at just around $1 million, which was about 33% growth off the very low Q4 2023 period, but also down a little sequentially from Q3 2024. For the full year, total revenue was down more than half a million dollars, a double digit percentage decline. Full results can be seen in the 10-K filing that was also released recently.

At this point, the company has a very high expense base. Trump Media had an operating loss of more than $45 million alone in Q4, and the company lost over $400 million for the year when including all costs from going public and the change in value of derivative liabilities. At this point, the main money maker is actually interest from the cash on the balance sheet, with interest income almost hitting $15 million in 2024, several times what was reported in actual revenue.

As I've continued to detail, this company does have a healthy balance sheet. Between the SPAC merger as well as equity sales, Trump Media finished last year with roughly $777 million in cash and investments, while having less than $10 million of total debt. Total cash burn last year was about $66 million, excluding the $7 million spent on an acquisition. Should the company need additional funds, it has a Standby Equity Purchase Agreement ("SEPA") currently available that could bring in another roughly $2 billion in proceeds.

The one negative here is ongoing dilution, which really piled up throughout 2024. The outstanding share count finished last year at just over 220 million, after being at 137 million at the end of March 2024 right after the SPAC merger was finalized. There is a bit of stock based compensation ongoing here, with the potential for more dilution through the SEPA if need be, and I doubt we'll see any type of major share repurchase program anytime soon.

Let's do something different:

While the original goal was to focus on just being the top conservative media platform, Trump Media has taken things a step further. As the 8-k filing above details, the company is looking for potential mergers or acquisitions across all areas of business. The company has licensed its Truth.Fi brand to an investment advisor, through which it plans to launch a set of separately managed accounts (“SMAs”) in cooperation with Charles Schwab (SCHW), as well as a slate of exchange traded funds (“ETFs”) that will reflect America First principles.

There will certainly be a lot going on here throughout 2025. It would not surprise me to see the company use some of that cash balance to buyout a smaller firm or two that meets its long term strategies targeting the right side of the political spectrum. Once some of these other ventures start to get going the company should start generating some level of revenue. However, I should remind everyone that the end of next month marks a year since the SPAC merger closed and we still don't have any analyst estimates for this company. Thus, it is hard to even guess how much revenue potential is actually out there at the moment.

Trying to figure out a proper valuation for DJT stock:

Based on the latest reported share count from the above 10-K, and Tuesday's closing price, Trump Media finished Tuesday with a market cap of just over $6.43 billion. If you use the revenues generated for the full year in 2024, that would put the name at roughly 1,778 times its sales. Even if revenues finally start to soar with these new streaming and financials services platforms built out, you're still looking at a very expensive stock that would be trading at several dozen or even a few hundred times its sales.

As a point of reference, social media giant and Facebook / Instagram parent Meta Platforms (META) goes for just about 10 times its expected 2025 revenues, while a smaller name like Rumble Inc. (RUM) goes for about 27.5 times. For argument's sake, let's say Trump Media were to double the Rumble valuation for this year because Trump Media likely would have much more revenue growth on a percentage basis. In that scenario, Trump Media would need about $117 million in yearly revenues just to trade at that nearly 55 times sales multiple, which itself would still be quite high.

Final thoughts and recommendation:

With another quarter of near zero revenues on the books, Trump Media finds itself in an even more interesting place than it was a year ago. In early 2024, investors were hoping that the former President's brand would lead to a potential media empire, but we haven't seen anything close to that just yet. While the streaming platform hasn't been fully rolled out just yet, revenues for the company declined last year and now the business is pivoting towards financial services.

The balance sheet here is certainly healthy, but this company trades at such a high valuation that any sizable mistakes moving forward could really hurt shareholders. That's why I am maintaining my sell rating on the stock today, until we have at least some idea of how big this business can actually be. For now, Trump Media remains in the prove it stage, but investors are hoping that maybe changes at some point this year.

data.title: My Dividend Stock Portfolio: New January Dividend Record - 100 Holdings With 14 Buys
data.description:
Portfolio Changes in January

Investment activity in January remained consistent with previous months as I added around $1,500 in net capital with almost all of that allocated to Business Development Companies.

My main picks were the Owl Rock Capital (OBDC), Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP) and W.P. Carey (WPC).

In January, all my moves added $126 to my yearly dividend income to start off the new year with a solid level. My aim is to lift my dividend income by about $100 each month, mostly doable thanks to the high yields from BDCs, and I am currently far ahead of that goal.

Overall, the average yield on cost of my new investments in January averaged around 8.5%, reinforcing my belief in BDCs as a core part of my portfolio. I'm banking on their dividends staying steady - that's what I'm all about when it comes to generating income. Time will tell if I'm on the money or just overly optimistic.

All these purchases break down as follows:

All net purchases in January can be found below:

Dividend Income in January 2025

Dividend income hit a new blistering January record of $814. This represents growth of 21% Y/Y and 8% sequentially. These figures are more than impressive to me and mostly boosted by the relatively recent aggressive additions of Goldman Sachs BDC (GSBD) and Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL). My intention is to continue to build these positions into the $5,000 to $10,000 range and ideally reach a level of around $1,000 in dividends per position per year as a mid-to-long-term goal.

In alignment with my investment strategy, I've incorporated a BDC/Non-BDC breakdown into my dividend income charts. This addition allows for more precise monitoring of this critical development.

Additionally, I have created a new visualization that dissects dividend income into two distinct categories: BDCs and Non-BDCs. These categories in turn are separated by dividend payment frequency into Monthly (blue) and Quarterly (orange) in absolute and relative terms.

For Q1 2024, BDCs accounted for only 22% of my dividend income making it a quarter that is not focused on BDCs. That is expected to drop further in February and then likely double in March where most of the BDCs are paying including any special dividends and Hercules Capital which follows an irregular dividend schedule.

This metric provides valuable insights and will help guide my investment decisions as it evolves over time.

In January, dividend income from my top three regular payers reached $260, a big jump from last year's average of $233 and getting closer to the $300 level. This is mainly driven by ongoing purchases of W. P. Carey (WPC), Rithm Capital (RITM), Goldman Sachs BDC, Altria (MO) and Blackstone Secured Lending Fund.

All this is depicted below:

Here is a chart that shows the development of my net dividend income by month over time between 2015 and 2025. You can see the growth of my dividend income and the average annual dividend for each year:

This chart is my favorite because it illustrates the progression of my dividend income over time and allows me to easily see the average annual dividend for each year.

Next, I have plotted all the individual dividend payments I have received, coloring them by year and arranging the years side by side instead of horizontally as in previous updates. This visualization allows for a more comprehensive analysis of the distribution of my dividend payments over time.

By visualizing the data in this manner, we get a clear picture of how dividend payments are spread out over different years, offering valuable insights into the overall growth of my dividend income over the years.

This visualization is initially quite cluttered, but it contains a wealth of information. It shows every individual dividend payment I have received since I began my investment journey in 2015, represented as a circle that is colored and sized according to its contribution. The view is organized by month and year, allowing for a more comprehensive analysis of the development of my dividend income over time. For each year and month, a white rectangle indicates the average monthly dividend. The area where dividends fall below the average is filled in dark red, while the area above the average is colored dark green.

This type of data visualization allows for a detailed examination of the distribution of my dividend payments over time and enables me to easily identify trends and patterns in my income growth.

Now, zooming in on January, we can immediately see a number of big dark blue circles in a sea of black. The bigger the circle and the bigger the distance to the previous circle for the same stock, the bigger is the change in dividend income compared to the prior years.

The importance of W.P. Carey, Goldman Sachs BDC, Rithm Capital and Altria is very clear, with a reddish circle indicating the massive jump in dividend income over the last 12 months due to heavy ongoing monthly investments, and I have no plans to stop them either via monthly savings plans or on an ad-hoc basis up to several times a week.

Apart from that, when it comes to the dividends for 2025, I notice mostly blue circles at the top of the scale, which is exactly what I want to see. These big blue circles represent a rising dividend income, and my goal is to have as many of them as possible at the top of the chart.

Overall, my aim is to observe numerous large orange circles positioned at the highest point on the scale. This signifies a positive trend of increasing dividend income, which is precisely what I'm striving for.

Another way to express the monthly dividend income is in terms of Gifted Working Time (GWT). Assuming an average hourly rate of $37.34 for 2025, my GWT and annual net dividends have been as follows:

  • 2018: 121 hours GWT, $3,000 in annual net dividends
  • 2019: 142 hours GWT, $3,600 in annual net dividends
  • 2020: 152 hours GWT, $3,800 in annual net dividends
  • 2021: 180 hours GWT, $5,050 in annual net dividends
  • 2022: 229 hours GWT, $6,400 in annual net dividends
  • 2023: 272 hours GWT, $8,800 in annual net dividends
  • 2024: 284 hours GWT, $10,200 in annual net dividends
  • 2025: Targeting at least 295 hours GWT, $11,000 in annual net dividends

The overall target for the year with $11,000 in annual net dividends is very conservative, but I expect that I will have to take out some risk from my portfolio as I am eyeing to raise capital for real estate investments if I encounter the right deal and definitely for a lot of traveling. This would represent a growth rate of around 8% which admittedly is a low target but definitely something I am willing to update as the year unfolds. If things go differently the dream target would be to pass the next milestone of $12,000 which would require around 18% dividend growth.

That would be a great achievement but nothing I am banking on to reach this year - it would be the icing on the cake.

Across the years, my YTD Dividend Race has been as follows:

  • 2018: Disappointing
  • 2019: Phenomenal, benefiting from a low baseline in the prior year
  • 2020: Fairly disappointing
  • 2021: Phenomenal
  • 2022: Much better than expected, fueled by the very weak euro
  • 2023: Breathtaking
  • 2024: Simply great

I have updated the YTD Dividend Race chart to show the development of YTD dividend income on a daily basis, allowing us to see that most of my dividend income is generated mid-month and towards the end of the month. The chart also breaks down the development by month for the current year, making it easy to identify significant jumps in income.

Expressed in GWT, it presents itself as follows:

What this shows is as follows:

  1. All time (blue area) - Around 1,514 hours, or 189 days, of active work have been replaced with passive income since the start of my dividend journey. Assuming a five-day workweek, this translates into more than half a year in total.
  2. Full-year 2025 (green bars) - Around 22 hours, or 2.7 days, of active work have been replaced with passive income in 2025, which is basically equivalent to half a working week funded with dividends. That is great progress, and I can't wait to add another month to that statistic.
  3. Highlighted in pink is the accumulated total at the end of the current reporting month (January).

This visualization allows us to see the overall impact of my dividend income on the amount of active work I am able to replace with passive income. It also enables us to see the progress made year-to-date, as well as the accumulated total at the end of the current reporting month.

Upcoming February Dividends

Contrary to January, February features significantly fewer dividend payers but among them are the big dividends from AT&T (T) and AbbVie (ABBV). Beyond that dividend payments are overall concentrated in the first half of the month with only one meaningful dividend payment from the Royal Bank of Canada (RY) expected in the second half of February.
